title: Commercial Roofing in Gaffney, SC | Commercial Roofers of Spartanburg

html:
Manufacturing and Retail Roof Specifications on the Gaffney I-85 Corridor

Gaffney's commercial roofing splits into two distinct categories along the same stretch of I-85: the large-span manufacturing roof over the Freightliner chassis plant and its supporting buildings, and the low-slope retail roofs across the outlet marketplace near the exit. We keep the specification approach for each separate, since the failure modes, codes, and warranty structures involved don't overlap much between the two building types.

Large-Span Standing Seam Roofing for Heavy Manufacturing

Chassis and heavy-truck manufacturing buildings need roof spans wide enough to clear crane and assembly-line equipment, which typically means standing seam metal roofing over a structural steel frame rather than a built-up or single-ply system on a shorter-span deck. We specify panel gauge and clip spacing against the building's actual wind uplift requirements, factoring in the open exposure common along this stretch of I-85, rather than using a standard commercial gauge across the board.

Thermal movement is a bigger design factor on a long standing seam run than most owners expect. Panel runs of significant length need expansion accommodation built into the clip system, and we detail that movement allowance based on the specific panel length and expected temperature swing rather than a generic rule of thumb.

Retail Strip Roofing at the Outlet Marketplace

The outlet marketplace near the I-85 exit is a collection of individually leased retail bays under a shared or adjacent roof structure, which creates a different coordination challenge than a single-tenant building. Roof work on one bay can affect drainage or membrane continuity on the bay next door, so we document the full roof's condition during any single-tenant scope rather than limiting the review to the leased area under contract.

Tenant improvement penetrations, from new HVAC curbs to signage conduit runs, accumulate over time across a retail strip like this one, and many were installed by trades without a roofing specialty. We check flashing quality at every existing penetration we encounter during a service call, since a poorly flashed prior tenant improvement is a common source of leaks attributed incorrectly to the base roof membrane.

Wind and Storm Exposure Along the Open I-85 Corridor

Both categories of building here sit in relatively open terrain along I-85 near the state line, which means wind exposure calculations lean toward the higher end of the applicable ASCE 7 category. We build that exposure assumption into uplift calculations for both the standing seam manufacturing roof and the retail strip's low-slope membrane, rather than assuming a lower exposure category based on the area's small-town character.

What Differs Between Manufacturing and Retail Roof Scopes Here

  • Panel gauge and clip spacing versus membrane thickness and fastening pattern
  • Thermal movement allowance on long standing seam runs
  • Shared drainage and membrane continuity across multi-tenant retail bays
  • Flashing condition at accumulated tenant improvement penetrations
  • Applicable wind exposure category for open I-85 corridor terrain
  • Crane and structural clearance requirements specific to manufacturing spans

Coordinating Roof Work With Retail Tenant Schedules

Outlet marketplace tenants generally can't accommodate a closed storefront during business hours, so exterior roof work at bay level gets scheduled around store hours where noise or access could disturb shoppers below. We confirm each tenant's operating schedule with the property manager before mobilizing rather than assuming a single blanket schedule applies across every bay in the strip.

Parking lot logistics also factor into scheduling here, since the outlet marketplace draws a meaningful amount of highway traffic pulling off I-85 on weekends. We plan material staging and crane placement around the busiest traffic periods where the schedule allows, favoring weekday mobilization when lot volume and shopper counts are typically lower.

Questions Owners and Facility Managers Ask

Why does a chassis manufacturing building need standing seam metal instead of single-ply membrane?

The wide spans required to clear crane and assembly-line equipment are typically framed in structural steel suited to standing seam metal roofing rather than the shorter-span decking common under built-up or single-ply systems. We specify panel gauge and clip spacing against the building's actual uplift requirements for that span.

How do you handle thermal movement on long standing seam panel runs?

We calculate expansion accommodation in the clip system based on the specific panel length and expected temperature swing for the building, rather than applying a generic movement allowance that may not fit a run of this length across a large manufacturing roof.

Does roof work on one outlet marketplace bay affect neighboring tenants?

It can, since bays often share or adjoin the same roof structure. We document the full roof's condition during any single-bay scope, beyond the leased area under contract alone, and we check drainage continuity across the shared structure before finalizing any repair.

Why are tenant improvement penetrations a common leak source at the outlet marketplace?

Many HVAC curbs, signage conduits, and other penetrations were installed by trades without roofing specialty over the years. We inspect flashing at every existing penetration we encounter, since a poorly flashed prior installation is often mistaken for a base membrane failure.

How does wind exposure along I-85 affect roof design in Gaffney?

Open terrain along this stretch of I-85 pushes the applicable ASCE 7 wind exposure category higher than a sheltered site would see, and we build that into uplift calculations for both the manufacturing roof and the retail strip rather than assuming a lower category based on the area's scale. That calculation directly affects fastener spacing and perimeter detailing on both building types.
